skill.name: error-conversion-guide
skill.description: Guides Rust developers through error conversion patterns, From trait implementations, thiserror usage, and effective use of the question mark operator.
skill.summary: Convert Rust errors cleanly with From, thiserror, map_err, and the question mark operator.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many apparent command execution, network, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found these are Markdown and Rust documentation examples, not executable skill code; no malicious intent or prompt-injection attempt was found.

low_findings.title: Static command-execution indicators are Markdown false positives
low_findings.description: The flagged external command findings are caused by inline backticks and fenced Rust code examples, such as map_err and Result snippets. The skill only allows Read and Grep and contains no script file or executable command invocation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is in Markdown documentation and Rust fenced examples, not runnable Ruby or shell code. The allowed-tools metadata limits operation to Read and Grep.

low_findings.title: Static network indicator is a Rust example false positive
low_findings.description: The network finding points to repository.fetch() inside a Rust service-layer example. It is illustrative code for error conversion and does not define a real Fetch API call or external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged call appears inside a fenced Rust example with placeholder types. No URL, credential, or executable network client is present.

low_findings.title: Weak-cryptography and reconnaissance blockers are scanner false positives
low_findings.description: The high-risk weak cryptography hits at line 3 and the reconnaissance hit at line 358 do not correspond to cryptographic code or system inspection. They are ordinary description text and an error message example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is skill metadata about Rust error conversion, and line 358 is a DomainError message string. Neither location contains cryptographic algorithms, system commands, or host reconnaissance logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Executable-looking snippets in documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes Rust snippets that show filesystem reads and placeholder API operations. These are examples for error conversion and should remain clearly documented as illustrative code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippets are not executed by the skill, but they describe operations that would touch files or services if copied into a project. This is a documentation risk rather than a confirmed exploit.

content.value_statement: Rust projects often collect mismatched error types as they grow. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code suggest clear conversion patterns using From, thiserror, map_err, and anyhow.
actual_capabilities: Identifies when manual map_err calls can use automatic From conversions.
Explains how to derive thiserror variants with #[from] and #[source].
Shows when to implement From manually for custom conversion logic.
Compares map_err, anyhow, boxed errors, and typed error enums.
Recommends layered error boundaries for domain and infrastructure code.
Points out common Rust error conversion mistakes and safer alternatives.
limitations: It is a documentation-only skill and does not compile or test Rust code.
Examples use placeholder types that must be adapted to each project.
It does not replace a full architecture review for large error hierarchies.
It focuses on Rust error conversion, not general Rust performance or async design.

use_cases.title: Clean up service error types
use_cases.description: A backend developer can replace repeated manual conversions with a unified service error enum.

use_cases.title: Design library error APIs
use_cases.description: A library maintainer can choose typed errors, sources, and From implementations that preserve caller control.

use_cases.title: Teach idiomatic error handling
use_cases.description: A mentor can explain why the question mark operator requires compatible error conversions.

prompt_templates.title: Find simple conversions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust function and identify any manual error conversions that could use #[from] or the question mark operator.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an error pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Selecting between map_err, thiserror, and anyhow
prompt_templates.prompt: Given these Rust error types and call sites, recommend whether to use #[from], map_err, anyhow context, or a custom From implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Design a layered error model
prompt_templates.scenario: Application with domain and infrastructure errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design Rust error conversion boundaries between repository, domain, and service layers while preserving useful source errors.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an error hierarchy
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of a larger Rust module
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Rust error hierarchy for duplicate #[from] sources, lost error chains, unclear aliases, and conversions that hide important context.

output_examples.input: A function manually maps IO and parse errors into an application error.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend deriving an error enum with #[from] variants, then replacing repeated map_err calls with the question mark operator.

output_examples.input: A service combines database, validation, and external API failures.
output_examples.output: The skill would propose a unified service error type and explain where automatic conversion is appropriate.

output_examples.input: A project converts every error into a string before returning it.
output_examples.output: The skill would flag lost error chains and suggest preserving source errors through typed variants or contextual wrapping.

best_practices: Keep source errors when callers need debugging detail or matching behavior.
Use #[from] for simple one-source variants with no extra context.
Use map_err or context only where the call site adds meaningful information.
anti_patterns: Do not convert structured errors to strings when the source chain matters.
Do not add multiple #[from] variants for the same source error type.
Do not use boxed dynamic errors when callers need specific typed recovery.

faq.question: Does this skill run Rust code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for Rust error conversion patterns.

faq.question: When should I use thiserror?
faq.answer: Use thiserror when you want typed error enums and clear source preservation, especially in libraries.

faq.question: When should I use anyhow?
faq.answer: Use anyhow in application code when flexible errors and simple context are more useful than public typed errors.

faq.question: Why does the question mark operator fail with different error types?
faq.answer: The operator needs a conversion path from the source error into the function return error type.

faq.question: Can this help with layered Rust services?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to convert infrastructure errors into domain or application errors at clear boundaries.

faq.question: Will it choose the final architecture for my project?
faq.answer: No. It gives trade-offs and patterns, but project requirements should guide the final design.
